coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots using bike pollutant soot: Evaluation of structural, optical and moisture sensing properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Chaudhary, Priyanka
Verma, Arpit
Mishra, Akash
Yadav, Deepankar
Pal, Krishna
Yadav, B.C.
Ranjith Kumar, E.
Thapa, Khem B.
Mishra, Surabhi
Dwivedi, D.K.
coredata.dc:description: Humidity sensor-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been prepared using waste bike pollutant soot. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using a UV–Visible light chamber, UV–Visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, TEM, and FTIR research. Initially, the sample was exposed in a closed chamber to UV–Visible light, which produced green luminescence. The photo luminescent spectrometer revealed the emission of green spectra as well as the approximate size of the CQDs. TEM and HRTEM confirmed the spherical shaped particles ∼2 nm in size. The FTIR spectra indicated the existence of a functional group. The sensing element was fabricated using the spin coating method on a borosilicate substrate and was used as a humidity sensor. Carbon quantum dots have excellent humidity sensing properties, as evidenced by measurements of sensitivity, ageing effect, repeatability, reaction, and recovery times. Many humidity sensors based on metal oxides, semiconductors, and polymers have been investigated and developed. The novelty of the work, electrical humidity sensor has been proposed using the waste pollutant soot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green source
Hydrothermal
Pollutant soot
Humidity sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental representation of formation of CQDs.
UV–Visible absorption spectra and Tauc plot inset inside the spectra of carbon quantum dots (c) Photoluminescence of carbon quantum dot which shows that Emission spectra at 420 nm.
(a–c) HRTEM and TEM images of carbon quantum dots and inset the size distribution graph respectively.
Humidity sensing characteristics relation of impedance corresponding to the %RH (a) primary layer (b) two layer (c&d) Third layer and Fourth layer.
(a) Repeatability curve (b) Response and recovery curve (c) Ageing effect (d) sensitivities histogram and average sensitivities (inset) in three region.

Humidity-sensing characteristics (a impedance corresponding with relative humidity curve at logarithmic scale for first layer (a) impedance corresponding with relative humidity curve at logarithmic scale for second layer (c & d) impedance corresponding with relative humidity curve for third layer and fourth shows the linear curve at logarithmic.
Electrical humidity sensing mechanism with sensing set-up.
Sensing parameters of carbon quantum dots with layer variation.
Other parameters of the sensing element.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots using bike pollutant soot: Evaluation of structural, optical and moisture sensing properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Humidity sensor-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been prepared using waste bike pollutant soot. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using a UV–Visible light chamber, UV–Visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, TEM, and FTIR research. Initially, the sample was exposed in a closed chamber to UV–Visible light, which produced green luminescence. The photo luminescent spectrometer revealed the emission of green spectra as well as the approximate size of the CQDs. TEM and HRTEM confirmed the spherical shaped particles ∼2 nm in size. The FTIR spectra indicated the existence of a functional group. The sensing element was fabricated using the spin coating method on a borosilicate substrate and was used as a humidity sensor. Carbon quantum dots have excellent humidity sensing properties, as evidenced by measurements of sensitivity, ageing effect, repeatability, reaction, and recovery times. Many humidity sensors based on metal oxides, semiconductors, and polymers have been investigated and developed. The novelty of the work, electrical humidity sensor has been proposed using the waste pollutant soot.

ce:section.ce:para: The movement of a large number of available vehicles in the world is the primary source of emissions in the ecosphere. The automobiles emit a slew of contaminants, including carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxide, hydrogen, automotive soot or particulate matter, ammonia, and sulphur dioxide, all of which are hazardous to our health and the atmosphere [1–3]. Vehicle soot is another major source of emissions in the atmosphere. When looking at the dissipation of cars and bikes, a dark substance can be seen. The soot feast in the air and on the crushed as the cars and bikes transfer, which is harmful to people and the environment [4]. Due to this result, there are various concerns related to global warming, low airborne excellence, a lack of international status, numerous health issues, reduced flight, and acidic shower [5].

The steamy harvests are difficult to deal with directly, but vehicle soot can be postponed for their immediate entry into the atmosphere by reusing the soot. Carbon is commonly used in a variety of applications due to its low toxicity and availability [6]. Among all carbon nanostructures, CQDs are a new form of nanostructure with specific properties other than nanostructures; they also have photoluminescence and the band gap varies with their size [7,8]. Larger quantum dots emit longer wavelengths, while smaller dots emit shorter wavelengths. carbon quantum dots, in essence, perform the same function as an atom in that they have quantum energy levels, but quantum dots made of the same substance can produce different colours due to their size property [9,10].

The Quantum confinement effect is observed in semiconductor nanocrystals due to their reducing size. The physical, chemical, and biological properties of the material changed from bulk phase to nanoscale. The transition from bulk materials to nanomaterials. Usually, in a semiconductor material, the charge carriers have several energy levels and these energy levels are near to each other, therefore it is termed as energy bands. The quantum confinement plays an important role in quantum dots, in which the semiconductor crystal size approaches materials to Bohr radius electron energy levels become discrete, and this requirement is fulfilled in all three-dimension semiconductor materials that become quantum dots [11–13]. carbon quantum dots have potential applications in chemical sensing, biosensing, photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices, cellular imaging, bioimaging, targeted drug delivery systems, and other fields [14–18]. Scientists are currently working on a biological approach for synthesizing quantum dots rather than a chemical method since it uses waste materials that are eco-friendly and non-toxic. In the current study, waste material was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots, which is both effective and environmentally friendly.

Since humidity affects the physical, chemical, and biological processes, the effect of humidity is so apparent that it can be seen immediately on our surroundings. Humidity sensing is crucial because it influences not only the business costs of the production process in industries, but also the health and safety of humans [19,20]. Moisture control is essential in many industries, such as semiconductor manufacturing, and it is also needed in the medical industry for many instrumentation processes [21], such as incubators, respiratory instruments, and pharmaceutical processing. Humidity regulation is crucial in the gas purification process, the textile and paper industries, and food processing [22]. The presence of water vapor in the air or other gases is measured while humidity is monitored. Absolute humidity, which defines the ratio of mass (vapor) to volume and is measured in gm/m3, humidity, which defines the ratio of mass (Vapor) to total mass and is measured in percentage, and PPM by Volume, which indicates the ratio of volume (vapor) to volume of dry gas [23–26].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from waste materials have recently piqued the interest of researchers. Carbon quantum dots have evolved along this path for their potential, optical properties, and excellent sensing applications [27,28]. CQDs have been synthesized from a variety of sources, including soya milk, sugarcane juice, coffee, egg yolk, and dextrin [1,29,30]. The subsequent CQDs exhibited a high PL property as well as strong water solubility. We present here, the creation of sustainable, non-toxic, and luminescent (CQDs) from bike soot pollutant. The experiment's beauty is that it synthesized quantum dots from waste materials and used the pollutant. Such carbon quantum dots are highly sensitive for humidity.

The contaminant soot was collected from the dissipate pipe of a two-wheeler (Honda Shine 2008 model) and was used as precursor (carbon source) for carbon quantum dots synthesis. Quartz crystal mortar pestle, DI water and Teflon lined autoclave were also used for this experiment.

CQDs were produced by hydrothermal route in which various processes are termed as collecting the soot from bike exhaust, this soot was grinded with mortar pestle for 1 h to break the clumps into fine powder, and this was sieved by 15 mm sieving paper to remove the contamination from powder as shown in Fig. 1 . After that 10 mL of distilled water was poured into carbon powder and put it into a Teflon Line autoclave at 160 °C for 10 h, therefore, dark grey colour solution was obtained and again sieved with 15 mm paper and 14 mL distilled water was added more to get the light brown coloured solution further this solution was put into centrifugal machine at 10,000 rpm for 15 min for particle separation and dark brown coloured CQDs solution was obtained which was kept at 4 °C for more application as humidity sensor.

For the sensing element, the glass substrates were used on which the quantum dots were dropped, firstly the substrate washed with a laboratory cleaner (Extron) and at that time washed in an ultrasonic bath by plunging them in the DI liquid followed by IPA and acetone for 25 min each. These washed glass substrates were dried in the oven at ∼100 °C for about 20 min. The fabrication of carbon quantum dots on the cleaned substrate was deposited via spin coating method at 3000 rpm and then kept on the hot plate at 50 °C for 20 min for drying process. The obtained thin film was employed for electrical humidity sensor.

TEM and HRTEM analysis were observed by instrument (JEOL-2100F), and confirmed the size and graphitic crystalline nature of the QDs. The optical properties of the quantum dots confirmed by UV–Visible Spectrometer (EVOLUTION 201), and photoluminescence (Flouorolog–FL3-11)). The colour of the quantum dot was observed in the presence of UV–Visible chamber, which confirm the luminescence property of carbon quantum dot. The 360 nm excitation wavelength was used for the analysis of PL spectra. The operating voltage for TEM analysis was 100 Kv.

First, we checked the luminescence property of carbon quantum dots in a UV–visible light chamber inset in Fig. 2 (a). The solution of carbon quantum dots is green in color. The absorption spectra in the visible range are shown in Fig. 2 (b). The carbon quantum dots were tested in the range 200 nm–1100 nm using a UV–Visible Spectrometer (EVOLUTION 201), and the absorption peak was found to be about 300 nm. The optical band gap is calculated by using following relationship
(
α h υ
)
(
1 /n
)
= A
(
h υ -
E g
)

α in the equation is absorption coefficient, A is constant, Eg is optical band gap of the material and n represents the kind of electronic shift shown by the material, its ½ for direct allowed transition. Tauc plot is drawn between (αhν)2 and hν. The intercept at α = 0, gives the value of the absorption edge [31]. The extrapolation of linear part of the (αhν)2 to the (αhν)2 = 0, where (E = Eg) is direct band gap. The calculated band gap is 4.35 eV.

The PL curve in Fig. 2(c) shows that the obtained absorption peak of carbon quantum dots is 420 nm, confirming that the dimension of fluorescent nanodots ranges from 3 to 8 nm, and the colour shifts towards the blue range. The 360 nm excitation wavelength was used for the analysis of PL spectra. The samples were placed in quartz cuvettes, the wavelength of excitation is showed the shorter than the onset of absorption of the sample, the PL spectrum shows a broad peak with an emission at 420 nm.

The FTIR analysis verified the functional groups of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 2 depicts the oxygen, hydroxyl, and other functional groups (d). As shown in curve, the peak 3755 cm−1 is dedicated to the broadening vibration of the hydroxyl group (OH). C–H, COO, CC, CH2 and C–O broadening peaks are assigned to be 2934, 1390, 1557, and 1038 cm−1 peaks in the ranges, respectively [32,33]. The 2324 and 1558 cm−1 peaks shown as a C–N band and an N–H band, respectively. The presence of out-of-plane winding vibrations of C–H benzene rings centered at about 770 cm−1 is observed, aromatic rings of the carbon quantum dots surface also present and shown in curve. The CO category recognized the prominent peak centered at 1747 cm−1. At 1210 cm−1, the alkoxy C–O–C stretching vibration can be perceived. All of these feature groups demonstrate the existence of functional groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots, which is also responsible for the humidity sensor.

In case of Carbon-Dots TEM analysis is very important characterization for the verification of size of particles, which provides knowledge about the particle's tropology, size spreading, and crystalline property. The (HRTEM) analysis was used to confirm the periodicity of the graphitic core as well as to replicate its crystalline form. The size distribution from 1 nm to 10 nm is shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the average size distribution bar graph is also shown inset in Fig. 3(a), confirming that the majority of the particles are ∼ 2 nm. The graphitic crystalline nature of the CQDs is revealed by the HRTEM picture.

The humidity-controlled chamber is the combination of glass. The prepared thin film was mounted and fixed inside the humidity chamber with connecting to the two copper wires, which are already connected to the Impedance Analyser (WAYNE KERR, 6440B). The humidity was controlled by using the two saturated solutions K2SO4/KOH, both solutions were used for the humidification and dehumidification respectively. The hygrometer (HTC-1) was used inside the chamber for the measurement of relative humidity inside the chamber. This method of observing the humidity is commonly known as Static Method.

The impedance humidity sensing characteristics of bike shoot pollutant carbon quantum dots were analyzed based on thin film sensing element on borosilicate glass substrate by spin coating technique. The sensing is a surface phenomenon that depends on the adsorption and desorption of water molecules interacted with the surface metal ions. Initially, the fabricated film of carbon quantum dots were dried and for the humidification the saturated potassium sulphate solution was kept inside the humidity chamber. The sensing data was measured in increasing mode at room temperature. Further the decreasing mode data was analyzed by dehumidification inside the chamber replacing former salt solution from saturated aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide. The impedance was measured by Impedance Analyser (Wayne Kerr Precession Component Analyser, 6440B) at an operating temperature of 300 K.

The presence of water vapor in the air have an effect on the usual idealized environment in all respects, whether it's the level of human comfort, the stated atmospheric condition for manufacturing industries, or the working laboratory condition. To monitor the presence of moisture in the air, we need a low-cost monitoring system that measures the presence of water vapor in the air, or we may state that a low-cost humidity sensor setup is required. As previously stated, humidity is greatly affected by ambient conditions; thus, humidity sensing devices are influenced by a variety of physical and chemical conditions and lose their original properties. There is the requirement for the material which is less influenced by surrounding conditions [34–36].

Sensitivity, ageing effect, repeatability, response and recovery times are all important parameters for humidity sensing characteristics. We may describe the sensing behaviour of the material using these sensing parameters. The fourth layers of sensing factor were used for the healthier consideration of the result of the humidity on carbon quantum dots.

From Fig. 4 (a–d) the curves display the variation of output with respect to (% RH). The sensitivity was calculated from the following equation [37] at three region 10–25% RH, 25–55% RH and 55–90% RH, where the average sensitivity of the carbon quantum dots thin as first layer, second layer, third layer and fourth layers was found as 3.236, 5.356, 9.222 and 13.164 MΩ/%RH [38]. The region wise sensitivities are tabulated in Table .1
(1)
S =
C h a n g e
i n
I m p e d a n c e
(
Δ z
)
C h a n g e
i n
r e l a t i v e
h u m i d i t y
(
Δ
% R H
)
Here, ΔZ is the variation in impedance (in MΩ) and Δ%RH is the relative change in the relative humidity. The good sensitivity is obtained due to functional groups present in carbon quantum dots.

The repeatability of the best sample for fourth layer adsorption was determined. It is also an important factor in the sensor area, and it is well-defined as the conversion of output when performed under the same conditions. The repeatability of sensing material is depicted in Fig. 5 (a), and it is known that the prepared carbon quantum dots are 97% reproducible. One more important sensing aspect is the stability effect, which demonstrates the nature of the sensor and is shown by compelling the measurement when the experiment is completed after a week, two weeks, and one week. The material's stability effect is depicted in Fig. 5(b), and it is shown that the nontoxic by waste stuff bike shoot carbon quantum dots film is very stable. With good humidity response, response and recovery times are both important factors for a good sensor, and it is specified as the time required to arrive at 90% of the final sensor output [39], and the time taken by the sensor output to return to its first value from 90% to 10% RH is referred to as the sensor's recovery time. According to curve Fig. 5, the response and recovery time of carbon quantum dots are 42s and 52s, respectively (c). The sensitivity histograms in three regions are shown in Fig. 5(d), and the average sensitivity of the best samples of carbon quantum dots is shown in the inset. Table 2 lists all of the sensing parameters.

The impedance versus relative curves shows the nonlinearity, but from Fig. 6 (a,b,c,d) shows that the humidity sensing characteristic of the carbon quantum dots at logarithmic scale display the linear. The linear coordinate curve plotted in y-axis at the logarithmic scale, the sensing curve displays linear in Fig. 6 (a,b,c,d) for one layer,two layer, three layer and fourth layer. The maximum linearity has been observed for second and fourth layer.

Grotthuss chain reaction governs the impedance-based humidity sensing system (Fig. 7 ). Initially, the sensing film was free of water molecules, and when the moisture level within the chamber increased due to water molecules and the sensing film, the grotthuss chain mechanism reaction began. At lower humidity levels, water adsorption on the sensing surface begins, and the hydroxyl group attaches to the metal on the sensing portion, forming a chemisorbed process [40]. The residual H+ ions combine with the other O2− groups on the surface to form an additional hydroxyl group. The formation of physisorbed occurs in higher humidity regions. With advance growth in the RH stage, the next several layers of physisorbed water molecules are formed over hydrogen bonding [41,42]. Because of the hopping power of the proton present on the surface of the humidity sensing part, these many physisorbed layers are attached with weakly van der waals forces and are transportable. As a result, they are capable of forming constant dipoles between the two electrodes and the electrical conductivity with the water molecule adsorption was possible.

One of the dangerous contaminants, bike pollutant soot, has been discovered as a preliminary material for CQDs synthesis using a simple hydrothermal process. Using a hydrothermal treatment system, carbon quantum dots are synthesized from pollutant soot from bicycles. The several characterization methods to validate the dots. First, a UV light chamber verified the green fluorescence of carbon quantum dots; then, PL (Photoluminescence spectroscopy) analysis revealed an absorption peak at 420 nm, confirming that the quantum dots size is ∼6 nm. The energy band gap was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and estimated using a Tauc plot, and it was found to be 4.35 eV. The fabricated film was used for an impedance-based electrical humidity sensor. The average sensitivity of carbon quantum analyzed dots thin as first, second, third, and fourth layers was found to be 3.236, 5.356, 9.222, and 13.164 MΩ/%RH with ∼97% reproducibility. The quantum dots-based humidity sensor's response and recovery times were found to be 42 s and 52 s, respectively. The aging effect was investigated, which revealed that the material was very stable. The electrical humidity sensing of the pre-synthesized material was extensively investigated and yielded promising results. The response time was also satisfactory, and the pre-synthesized material was electrical in nature.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no Conflict of Interest.
